text: [Page 1] Fundamentals of Deep Learning(Assignment-1) Q1. Compare Deep Learning and Shallow Machine Learning using real datasets. Task: 1. Select any dataset (Iris, MNIST subset, or any CSV dataset). 2. Train a shallow ML model (e.g., logistic regression, decision tree). 3. Train a simple deep neural network on the same dataset. Compare: Accuracy Training time Overfitting behavior Feature engineering requirement Deliverables: Code Accuracy comparison table 5–7 line conclusion Q.2 Demonstrate Gradient Descent and Batch Optimization. Task: Implement Gradient Descent, Stochastic Gradient Descent, and Mini-Batch Gradient Descent for a simple regression task. Compare convergence speed and stability. Deliverables: Code Graphs of all optimization techniques Short analysis (6–8 lines) Q.3 Simulate a Biological Neuron vs. Artificial Neuron. Task: Draw a diagram (handmade or digital) comparing biological neuron components with artificial neuron components. Write Python code to implement an artificial neuron with: [Page 2] Weighted sum Activation function (ReLU/Sigmoid) Deliverables: Diagram Code + sample input/output Explanation of similarities/differences Build a Single-Layer Perceptron for a Binary Classification Task. Task: Train a perceptron on a linearly separable dataset. Plot the learned decision boundary. Deliverables: Code Decision boundary figure Summary of training process [Page 3] Fundamentals of Deep Learning(Assignment-1) Q1. Compare Deep Learning and Shallow Machine Learning using real datasets.  Shallow Machine Learning (Decision Tree) CODE: Overﬁƫng and Feature SelecƟon Overview : The model demonstrates clear overﬁƫng because a deep decision tree (max_depth = 20) is trained on raw MNIST pixel data containing 784 input features per image. This high model complexity allows the tree to learn very detailed and dataset-speciﬁc paƩerns, including noise, rather than learning generalizable digit representaƟons. As a result, the model tends to memorize training samples instead of capturing meaningful structures, which limits its ability to generalize to unseen test data. This behavior highlights the importance of feature selecƟon, since many

to learn very detailed and dataset-speciﬁc paƩerns, including noise, rather than learning generalizable digit representaƟons. As a result, the model tends to memorize training samples instead of capturing meaningful structures, which limits its ability to generalize to unseen test data. This behavior highlights the importance of feature selecƟon, since many pixel features are redundant, highly correlated, or irrelevant (e.g., background pixels). Retaining all features increases model complexity and overﬁƫng risk, whereas selecƟng or reducing features would improve generalizaƟon, reduce noise, and lead to more stable and eﬃcient learning performance. [Page 4]  Deep Learning CODE: Overﬁƫng and Feature SelecƟon Overview: This deep neural network shows much beƩer generalizaƟon behavior compared to the decision tree because it learns hierarchical feature representaƟons from the raw pixel data instead of relying on direct pixel-level splits. The mulƟ-layer structure with nonlinear acƟvaƟons (ReLU) enables the model to automaƟcally extract meaningful paƩerns such as edges, shapes, and digit structures, which allows it to generalize well to unseen data, as reﬂected by the high test accuracy. Although the model sƟll uses all 784 pixel features, it does not suﬀer from severe overﬁƫng because the learning process focuses on shared paƩerns across samples rather than memorizaƟon. In this case, explicit feature selecƟon is less criƟcal, since the neural network performs implicit feature learning through its hidden layers. However, dimensionality reducƟon or feature engineering could sƟll improve eﬃciency and training Ɵme, even though generalizaƟon performance is already strong. [Page 5] CONCLUSION: Overall, the two models demonstrate clear diﬀerences in learning behaviour and generalizaƟon performance. The decision tree model exhibits strong overﬁƫng due to its high depth and training on high-dimensional raw pixel data, which leads it to memorize noise and dataset-speciﬁc paƩerns rather than learning generalizable digit representaƟons. This results in limited generalizaƟon performance and highlights the importance

in learning behaviour and generalizaƟon performance. The decision tree model exhibits strong overﬁƫng due to its high depth and training on high-dimensional raw pixel data, which leads it to memorize noise and dataset-speciﬁc paƩerns rather than learning generalizable digit representaƟons. This results in limited generalizaƟon performance and highlights the importance of controlling model complexity and applying feature selecƟon or dimensionality reducƟon. In contrast, the deep neural network model achieves signiﬁcantly higher accuracy by learning hierarchical feature representaƟons directly from the data, enabling it to extract meaningful structures such as edges, shapes, and digit paƩerns. This allows the neural network to generalize eﬀecƟvely to unseen data without explicit feature selecƟon, as feature learning is performed implicitly through its layered architecture. Together, these results show that model choice and data representaƟon play a criƟcal role in performance: simple models with high- dimensional raw features are prone to overﬁƫng, while deep learning models are beƩer suited for complex data such as images due to their ability to learn robust and generalizable features automaƟcally. Q.2 Demonstrate Gradient Descent and Batch OpƟmizaƟon. -> Gradient Descent [Page 6] -> StochasƟc Gradient Descent [Page 7] -> Mini Batch Gradient Descent [Page 8] Conclusion: The three gradient descent methods represent diﬀerent strategies for minimizing a cost funcƟon. Batch Gradient Descent updates the model using the enƟre dataset at each step, which produces stable and smooth convergence but results in slower updates and higher computaƟon per iteraƟon. StochasƟc Gradient Descent updates the model using only one randomly selected data point at a Ɵme, which leads to faster updates but introduces high variability and ﬂuctuaƟons in the cost funcƟon, producing a noisy and uneven convergence curve. Mini-Batch Gradient Descent provides a balanced approach by using small subsets of data for each update, achieving a good compromise between speed and stability.

point at a Ɵme, which leads to faster updates but introduces high variability and ﬂuctuaƟons in the cost funcƟon, producing a noisy and uneven convergence curve. Mini-Batch Gradient Descent provides a balanced approach by using small subsets of data for each update, achieving a good compromise between speed and stability. It typically converges faster than SGD with smoother behavior, while being more eﬃcient than BGD, making it the most pracƟcal and scalable choice for large datasets.
